data.title: Warner Bros/Paramount deal paused by judge until mid-August
data.description:
The delay of Paramount Skydance's (PSKY) planned $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) was extended until mid-August, the judge overseeing the state attorneys general's attempt to block the deal wrote. 

The mega media deal is now delayed until at least Aug. 17,  Judge Araceli Martinez-Olguin wrote in a court filing on Thursday. On Monday, the judge ordered the companies not to close the deal for 14 days.

An Aug. 3 hearing on a preliminary injunction to delay the deal until a potential trial next year to permanently block the transaction is still scheduled. 

The states also told the judge that the parties are having “productive discussions” regarding the preliminary injunction, according to a filing late Thursday. The parties are expected to file a joint update to the court by noon Pacific time on Friday.

Shares of Warner Bros. (WBD) rose 1% on Friday.

If the closing does not take place by September 30, Paramount (PSKY) must pay an additional $0.25 (about $650M per quarter) to Warner Bros. shareholders.

data.title: Intel's capex increase positive for Foundry business, chip equipment makers
data.description:
Intel (INTC) raising its capital expenditure could be a positive for its Foundry business as the company noted that CapEx, though broad-based, could be skewed towards fabs.  Meanwhile, Wedbush noted that the CapEx increase was also positive for chip equipment makers.

On Thursday, Intel raised its full-year 2026 capital expenditure forecast to $20B, from up to $18B.

Answering an analyst's question during the company earnings call about what the CapEx increase of nearly $3B implies for the company's Foundry customers, CFO David Zinsner said:

"CapEx is fairly broad-based. It's going to include advanced packaging. As Lip-Bu talked about, we're pretty excited about our prospects on EMIB-T. And so we will be investing in that. That said, the cost of a fab for the front end is much more expensive than a packaging facility. So it will be skewed towards the front end. But nevertheless, both of them will be important to us."

Analysts at Melius Research, according to Reuters, said that "the capex increase not only signals confidence in cash flow ​upside and demand visibility from long-term agreements for products, but also ​confidence that Foundry customers are coming (for packaging and 14A wafers)," 

Zinsner said that the company was raising 2026 CapEx due to strong customer demand signals and added that the vast majority would be spent across its U.S. network.

Earlier this week, Intel announced a partnership with Fortinet (FTNT) to develop Fortinet Security Processor 6, or SP6, to strengthen global supply chain.

"We're also aggressively locking in tool purchase orders from our vendors, accelerating our clean room build-outs and actively securing supply of substrates and memory," said Zinsner on Thursday.

He added that as the company looks back from 2021 through 2026, its total capital spending on tools and space in the U.S. is approaching $100B, significantly higher than any other semiconductor company over that time frame.

Intel Foundry profitability remained negative but improved. The company also noted that progress on its advanced semiconductor manufacturing technology 18A has been "very good."

However, Seeking Alpha analyst Louis Gerard noted that "the Foundry segment is still not really a well-working business as, yes it posted a revenue base of $5.77B, which is up 31% Y/Y but it still posted an operating loss of -$2.09B and with the intersegment eliminations were set to -$5.48B so external foundry revenue only stood at a mere $300MM. And to be fair, the whole bull thesis behind INTC is about 18A external traction which has not been reflected in the earnings."

Meanwhile, Wedbush noted that Intel's CapEx increases were positive for semiconductor capital equipment makers. Analyst Matt Bryson said that Intel, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), and Micron Technology (MU) have now all increased spending plans.

Top chip equipment makers include ASML (ASML), Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX), and KLA (KLAC).

Shares of Intel fell about 2% on Friday.

data.title: This is a ‘winner takes it all market’ when it comes to AI investments – Constellation Research’s Ray Wang
data.description:
The market is actively seeking reasons to turn bearish on AI investments, even as tech giants continue to pour billions into an increasingly competitive landscape, according to Ray Wang, Constellation Research chairman and AI Forum co-founder.

In an interview with CNBC, Wang argued that despite investor skepticism following elevated capital expenditure announcements, major technology companies have no choice but to keep spending aggressively.

"This is a winner-takes-all market," Wang said. "And if you're not making that investment, you're going to be falling behind."

Wang noted that the so-called “Magnificent Seven” tech companies—(NVDA), (MSFT), (AAPL), (GOOGL), (AMZN), (META), (TSLA)—are approaching their AI spending strategies differently.

Amazon (AMZN), he explained, has never prioritized short-term gains over long-term benefits and will continue making substantial investments. Meta (META) similarly shows no hesitation in ramping up spending, while Microsoft (MSFT) "probably will be the most cautious of the Mag 7."

These companies can sustain their aggressive spending because of strong underlying businesses—Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG) benefit from Google Cloud growing 80 to 90 percent and solid YouTube revenues, while Meta's (META) advertising business continues to perform well.

The analyst characterized the current AI investment environment as "one of the biggest wealth transfers between what you had as asset light to asset heavy," with hardware vendors emerging as the clear winners.

He also pointed to "AI inflation," noting that prices across the AI supply chain continue to rise, adding pressure to already substantial capital outlays.

Wang highlighted a critical bottleneck that many investors overlook: permitting for data centers and energy constraints are significantly delaying expected returns.

"Permitting for data centers is what's holding everyone up," he said, adding that these administrative and physical barriers are "stretching it out 18 to 24 months" for companies forecasting their return on investment.

On stock valuations, Wang suggested that while Nvidia's (NVDA) growth potential has largely been priced in, other beneficiaries still have room to run.

"AMD (AMD) still has a lot to go," he noted, citing continued opportunities in data centers and the prospect of lower-cost compute and energy over time.

Despite investor impatience and the prospect of interest rates remaining elevated, Wang expects companies to continue their aggressive spending.

"They're going to continue," he said, noting that firms like Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG) have the free cash flow to sustain their investments regardless of higher financing costs.

Investors remain restless, Wang acknowledged, "waiting for any news to actually jump and actually trade against."

However, the high volatility environment keeps traders engaged with these stocks, even as the timeline for AI payoffs extends further into the future.

data.title: Celestica Q2 earnings on deck: What to expect
data.description:
Celestica (CLS) is set to post second quarter earnings results on Monday, after markets close.

Wall Street expects the electronics manufacturing services company to post EPS of $2.31 on revenue of $4.39B, implying a rise of 51.9% during the quarter.

The company is benefiting from a surge in demand in the data center and AI sectors.

Earlier in April, it reported stronger-than-expected first-quarter results and raised its guidance.

Seeking Alpha analysts, Wall Street, and Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings are all bullish and rated the stock a Buy and above.

The stock has risen more than 11% so far this year, outperforming the 8% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

“I like this company's operating momentum and AI industry positioning, and I do notice the power of the supercycle outcome materializing. The 1.6T ramp can structurally support continued equity strength through 2027, but macro concerns are more nuanced,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Oliver Rodzianko.

“Although Q1 itself removed uncertainty around the floor's shape for 2027, Q2 is where I am expecting some more modeling that can be attempted based on management's indications for the next year—more details, and likely bullish ones, given the management's track record of raising guidance almost every quarter in 2025-26,” noted another recent Seeking Alpha analysis by The Alpha Analyst.

Over the last two years, Celestica has beaten both EPS and revenue estimates 100% of the time.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen 16 upward revisions and no downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen 13 upward revisions versus no downward moves.

data.title: Nvidia CEO Huang joins X, signs letter to Congress on open AI models
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang has joined the X (formerly Twitter) social network, the tech titan said on Friday.

In his first post, he revealed that he signed a letter to the U.S. Congress on open AI models and the need for American leadership in the space.

“AI will transform every industry, power every company, and be built by every country,” Huang wrote in his post. “Open models strengthen safety and cybersecurity, accelerate innovation and diffusion, and enable sovereignty. The world needs both frontier closed models and frontier open models.”

The three-page letter was signed by Nvidia, along with a host of other tech industry heavyweights, including Meta Platforms (META), Box (BOX), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Microsoft (MSFT), and others.

Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella also posted on X about the letter and said they are "essential to a healthy AI ecosystem," while adding they will "strengthen American competitiveness,"  expand economic opportunity, and protect national security.

“Our AI leadership will be judged not by one frontier AI model, but by whether the United States builds a strong, open ecosystem that diffuses into every sector,” the letter reads. “This is essential for creating opportunities for innovation and prosperity across the country. It requires expanding access to AI, encouraging competition, robust application layers, and giving Americans greater control over the technology they rely on. Open-weight models—AI models that anyone can download, inspect, modify, and run on their own infrastructure—are an important part of that foundation because they make advanced AI more accessible, adaptable, and widely available.”

This is breaking news...

data.title: 4 stocks to watch on Friday: VZ, LLY, ORCL, DELL
data.description:
Stock index futures were higher on Friday as the stock market hoped to recover from Thursday's selloff.

Here are four stocks to watch on the day: 

Oracle (ORCL) shares rose 2.33% in premarket trading after the software and cloud company secured a 10-year contract with the U.S. Department of War to provide software services in a deal worth up to $6.99 billion if all option years are exercised. The contract covers Oracle software for on-premise data centers across all branches of the U.S. military, the Coast Guard, and the U.S. intelligence community.

Verizon Communications (VZ) shares fell 0.94% in early trading after the telecom giant reported mixed second-quarter results. Adjusted earnings topped Wall Street estimates, while revenue came in below expectations. Verizon also raised its full-year guidance for the second consecutive quarter, citing strong subscriber growth and record profitability.

Dell Technologies (DELL) shares rose 0.95% in premarket trading after Citi placed the technology company on a 90-day upside catalyst watch ahead of its July-quarter earnings report. The brokerage said enterprise IT infrastructure spending continues to strengthen, supported by artificial intelligence investments, server refresh cycles, and broader demand for data center capacity.

Eli Lilly (LLY) shares fell 0.24% in early trading after Novo Nordisk said it filed a preliminary injunction in a U.S. federal court seeking to immediately block Lilly from running advertisements for its obesity and diabetes medicines. The filing comes days after Novo sued its U.S. rival, alleging Lilly's advertising campaign for its GLP-1 products misled American consumers
